title: Reading and Use of English – Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Evolution of Language in the Digital Age
content.subtitle: How the rapid pace of online communication is reshaping the way we think and connect.
content.text: The history of language is a history of adaptation, but never before has it evolved at the dizzying speed we see today. In the pre-internet era, linguistic shifts—from the introduction of new vocabulary to changes in grammatical structure—took decades or even centuries to permeate the mainstream. Now, a single viral meme or a popular hashtag can alter the global lexicon in a matter of days. [31] The writer suggests that this 'linguistic acceleration' is not merely a change in the words we use, but a reflection of a deeper shift in how we process and categorize information. [/31] We are moving toward a more visual, condensed, and emotive form of communication, where brevity is prioritized over nuance. || This shift is most visible in the rise of 'internet slang' and emojis. While critics often dismiss these as a degradation of language, some linguists argue that they represent a sophisticated new form of 'digital punctuation.' [32] Emojis, for instance, serve as vital 'contextual markers,' providing the emotional tone that is often lost in text-based communication. [/32] They help to prevent the misunderstandings that occur when sarcasm or irony is stripped of its vocal inflection. In this sense, digital language is becoming more like spoken language—fluid, informal, and deeply dependent on social context. We are not losing the ability to communicate; we are developing a new set of tools to do so in a faster, more globalized environment. || [33] The author's tone when discussing the 'democratization' of language is one of cautious optimism. [/33] In the past, lexicographers and academics acted as the 'gatekeepers' of language, deciding which words were 'correct' and which were 'slang.' Today, the power has shifted to the users. Language is being shaped from the bottom up, by teenagers, marginalized communities, and internet subcultures. This allows for a more inclusive and diverse range of expression, but it also leads to 'linguistic fragmentation,' where different online 'bubbles' develop their own exclusive dialects. The challenge is to maintain a common ground while celebrating this newfound creativity. || Another concern is the impact of predictive text and AI-driven writing tools. [34] These systems are designed to suggest the 'most likely' next word, which can lead to a homogenization of thought and expression. [/34] If we all rely on the same algorithms to complete our sentences, we risk losing the idiosyncratic 'voice' that makes human writing unique. We are being nudged toward the path of least resistance, favoring the cliché over the original thought. This 'algorithmic drift' may be making our communication more efficient, but at the cost of its creative depth. || [35] However, some researchers argue that the digital age is actually fostering a 'new literacy.' [/35] They suggest that the average person today writes more than at any other time in history—from emails and blog posts to social media captions. This constant practice, even if informal, is developing a generation that is highly sensitive to audience and tone. We are becoming 'code-switchers,' capable of moving seamlessly between the formal language of a job application and the highly coded language of a group chat. This linguistic flexibility is a valuable skill in a complex, multi-modal world. || Ultimately, the future of language is as unpredictable as the technology that drives it. [36] The writer concludes that while the medium of communication may change, the fundamental human desire for connection and self-expression remains constant. [/36] We must learn to navigate the digital landscape without losing sight of the richness and complexity that language provides. Whether we are using an emoji or a sonnet, the goal is the same: to be understood. The evolution of language is not a sign of its decline, but a testament to its enduring vitality.

questions.question: In the first paragraph, what does the writer suggest about 'linguistic acceleration'?
options: A) It is primarily a problem for people who do not use the internet.
B) It indicates that human intelligence is declining at a similar rate.
C) It reflects a fundamental change in our cognitive processes.
D) It is a temporary trend that will slow down once technology matures.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [31], stating it's a reflection of a 'deeper shift in how we process... information.' A and D are not mentioned. B is an incorrect interpretation of the shift in processing.

questions.question: According to the second paragraph, emojis are useful because they...
options: A) are faster to type than traditional words and sentences.
B) allow people to communicate without needing to learn a language.
C) provide the emotional context that text often lacks.
D) are the only way to express irony in a digital environment.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [32] and the mention of 'contextual markers' for emotional tone. A is a minor point, not the main function described. B is not suggested. D is too extreme ('only way').

questions.question: What is the author's view on the shift in 'linguistic power' described in the third paragraph?
options: A) He regrets the loss of academic authority over language.
B) He is hopeful about the inclusivity it brings, but warns of fragmentation.
C) He believes that teenagers are the only ones capable of shaping language.
D) He thinks that online 'bubbles' are the best way to preserve dialects.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [33] and his tone of 'cautious optimism' regarding inclusivity and the 'challenge' of fragmentation. A is the opposite of his tone. C is too narrow. D misinterprets the danger of 'fragmentation'.

questions.question: The 'algorithmic drift' mentioned in the fourth paragraph can lead to...
options: A) a significant increase in the speed of professional writing.
B) a loss of individual personality in the way people write.
C) a decrease in the number of people who use social media.
D) an improvement in the grammatical accuracy of most users.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [34] and the risk of losing the 'idiosyncratic voice' and favoring the 'cliché.' A is a byproduct, not the main negative consequence described. C and D are not mentioned.

questions.question: What is the 'new literacy' referred to in the fifth paragraph?
options: A) The ability to read and write in multiple foreign languages.
B) The skill of communicating effectively using different styles and tones.
C) The requirement to be formal in all digital communications.
D) The tendency for people to write less and use more visual media.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [35] and the mention of 'code-switchers' moving between styles. A is not mentioned. C is the opposite of the author's point. D is contradicted by the statement that people write more now than ever.

questions.question: In the final paragraph, the writer concludes that language evolution is...
options: A) a sign that humans are losing their desire for deep connection.
B) an inevitable decline caused by the medium of communication.
C) a proof of the continued strength and importance of language.
D) something that can only be understood through technology.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [36] and the call it a 'testament to its enduring vitality.' A and B are the opposite of the conclusion. D is not suggested.
